items.q: What's the difference between monthly, quarterly, and yearly?
items.a: Monthly is a recurring subscription with a {monthlyTrialDays}-day free trial — cancel anytime. Quarterly includes a {quarterlyTrialDays}-day free trial, valid for 3 months with no auto-renewal. Yearly includes a {yearlyTrialDays}-day free trial, valid for one year with no auto-renewal.

items.q: Do I need a credit card for the free trial?
items.a: All plans require a card, securely processed via Stripe. Cancel during the trial period ({monthlyTrialDays} days for monthly / {quarterlyTrialDays} days for quarterly / {yearlyTrialDays} days for yearly) and you won't be charged.

Cancel anytime via the Stripe customer portal (click "Manage Subscription" on the pricing page). Cancel during the trial and you won't be charged. Paid users keep Pro access until the end of the billing cycle. Yearly plans do not auto-renew.
We accept Visa, Mastercard, American Express, and other major credit and debit cards, securely processed through Stripe (PCI DSS Level 1 certified).

items.q: Can you guarantee I'll reach CLB 7+?
items.a: We do not guarantee any exam results. HiTCF provides high-quality practice aligned with TCF Canada to help you identify and improve weak areas. Final scores depend on many factors. See our disclaimer for details.

items.q: Are these the same as the real exam questions?
items.a: Practice questions are compiled and edited from publicly available materials, covering levels A1–C2 and formatted to match TCF Canada. However, this platform is not officially affiliated, and questions are not identical to actual exam items.

items.q: How do I request a refund?

exam.title: What is TCF Canada
exam.desc: TCF Canada (Test de connaissance du français pour le Canada) is a French proficiency test developed by France Éducation international (formerly CIEP), designed specifically for Canadian immigration and citizenship applications.
speakingTasks: Role play (5.5 minutes, incl. 2 min prep)

scores.title: NCLC / CLB Score Conversion Table
scores.subtitle: Immigration, Refugees and Citizenship Canada (IRCC) uses NCLC / CLB levels to assess language proficiency. Below is the TCF Canada score conversion.
scores.footnote: * NCLC 7 is the minimum language requirement for most Express Entry and PNP programs, and the target level for most test-takers
scores.nclc7Desc: NCLC 7 (i.e. CLB 7) is the minimum French requirement for most Canadian economic immigration programs. Reaching NCLC 7 means you meet the language threshold for Express Entry (CEC, FSW) and most Provincial Nominee Programs (PNP). Higher NCLC levels (8, 9, 10) earn additional CRS points — NCLC 9 gives the maximum language bonus.

learn.title: Free French Study Resources
learn.subtitle: All free or official resources to help you improve your French from every angle

official.desc: Free French learning platform by the French international TV network. Video courses + interactive exercises for all levels from A1 to C1, organized by themes like culture, society, and science.
French international radio learning channel. Real French news audio with practice exercises. Various speech speeds from slow to normal — excellent TCF listening prep material.
RFI's 'Easy French News' — 10-minute clear, slow-paced daily international news broadcasts. Listen daily to steadily improve your listening comprehension.
Official TCF test organization website. View exam descriptions, scoring criteria, and official sample questions. Essential to understand the real exam format before preparing.
Long-established French learning site with extensive free grammar, vocabulary, listening and reading exercises graded by difficulty. Great for building solid grammar foundations.

Hugo's French learning platform with podcasts, YouTube videos, and courses. Natural French discussing cultural and social topics. Moderate pace with depth — ideal for intermediate learners to break through plateaus.

youtube.desc: Million-subscriber immersive French channel. Johan speaks only French about everyday topics — no grammar rules, just natural input to develop intuition.
French native Dylane's teaching channel focused on grammar explanations and vocabulary expansion. Short, clear, and well-organized episodes.
French teacher Pierre's comprehensive channel covering grammar, vocabulary, pronunciation, and culture. French audio with subtitles — perfect for intensive listening.
Hugo's pure French podcast channel. 20–30 minute episodes discussing cultural and social topics. Moderate pace, great for extensive listening and building speaking/writing material.
Street interview series in France. Real everyday French from native speakers with French-English subtitles. Authentic expressions and spoken rhythm you won't find in textbooks.
Bilingual English-French teaching channel. Alexa explains French grammar and pronunciation in English. Slow pace with lots of examples — perfect for absolute beginners up to A2.

tools.name: Bilibili
tools.desc: Search for 'French A1 textbook audio' or 'TCF prep' — tons of French learning videos with Chinese explanations. The most accessible resource for Chinese-speaking learners.

tools.name: Anki
tools.desc: Free, open-source spaced repetition flashcard app. Download community-shared TCF/DELF vocabulary decks. Scientific algorithms schedule reviews for far better long-term retention than cramming.

tools.name: Duolingo
tools.desc: Gamified language learning app with 10–15 minute daily lessons. While it won't match TCF depth, it's great for maintaining daily French exposure.

tools.name: LogicielÉducatif
tools.desc: French educational games website with mini-games for spelling, grammar, and vocabulary practice. Made for French students but equally effective for learners.

tools.name: Lawless French
tools.desc: Comprehensive French grammar reference site covering all grammar points from beginner to advanced with example sentences and exercises. Use it like a grammar dictionary.

infoCollect.authP1: Users register and log in via email. Through this process, we collect:
infoCollect.payP1: Paid subscriptions are processed through Stripe. We do not directly store your credit card numbers or bank card information. Stripe, as a PCI DSS Level 1 certified payment processor, independently manages your payment data. We only store:

infoCollect.usageP1: During your use of the Platform, we automatically collect:
infoCollect.usageLi1: Practice and exam records (answers, scores, time spent)
infoCollect.usageLi3: Practice statistics (accuracy rates, streak days, etc.)
infoCollect.cookieP1: The Platform uses the following cookies or similar technologies:

infoCollect.cookieP2: The Platform currently does not use third-party analytics tools (such as Google Analytics) or advertising tracking cookies. If introduced in the future, this policy will be updated and users will be notified.

purpose.title: 2. Purpose of Information Use
purpose.p1: Personal information we collect is used solely for:
purpose.li1: Providing and maintaining Platform services (authentication, practice records, performance statistics)
purpose.li2: Processing subscription payments and managing account status

purpose.p2: We will not use your personal information for advertising or sell it to third parties.

tcfCanada.heading: What Is TCF Canada? A Complete Guide
tcfCanada.intro: The TCF Canada (Test de connaissance du français pour le Canada) is the official French language proficiency test recognized by Immigration, Refugees and Citizenship Canada (IRCC). Whether you are applying for permanent residence or Canadian citizenship, understanding this exam is your first step toward success.

whatIs.title: What Is the TCF Canada Exam?
whatIs.p1: The TCF Canada is administered by France Education international (formerly CIEP) and is specifically designed for immigration purposes to Canada. Unlike the general TCF, the TCF Canada evaluates all four language skills: listening, reading, speaking, and writing. It provides results mapped to the Niveaux de compétence linguistique canadiens (NCLC), which are equivalent to the Canadian Language Benchmarks (CLB) used by IRCC.
whatIs.p2: The test is available at authorized test centers worldwide and can be taken multiple times. Results are valid for two years from the test date. Many candidates take the test more than once to improve their scores, making regular practice essential.

structure.title: Exam Structure: 4 Sections
structure.intro: The TCF Canada consists of four mandatory sections. The listening and reading sections are computer-based multiple choice, while speaking and writing are evaluated by certified examiners.

co.title: Listening Comprehension (Compréhension orale — CO)
co.desc: 39 multiple-choice questions to be completed in approximately 35 minutes. Questions progress from simple everyday dialogues (A1) to complex academic lectures and debates (C2). Each audio recording is played only once during the exam, so active listening skills are critical.

ce.title: Reading Comprehension (Compréhension écrite — CE)
ce.desc: 39 multiple-choice questions to be completed in approximately 60 minutes. Texts range from short signs and notices (A1) to lengthy academic or literary passages (C2). You will encounter advertisements, emails, newspaper articles, essays, and research abstracts.

eo.title: Speaking (Expression orale — EO)
eo.desc: 3 tasks completed in approximately 12 minutes, recorded and evaluated by examiners. Task 1 is a guided interview, Task 2 is a role-play interaction, and Task 3 requires expressing and defending an opinion on a complex topic.

ee.title: Writing (Expression écrite — EE)
ee.desc: 3 writing tasks to be completed in approximately 60 minutes. Task 1 is a short message or email (60-120 words), Task 2 is an article or letter on a given topic (120-150 words), and Task 3 is a comparative essay analyzing two perspectives (120-180 words).

scoring.title: Scoring System
scoring.p1: Each section is scored independently on a scale corresponding to NCLC levels 1 through 12+. For the listening and reading sections, a numerical score from 0 to 699 is assigned, which maps to a specific NCLC level. Speaking and writing are scored directly on the NCLC scale by trained evaluators.
scoring.p2: Your TCF Canada attestation will show four separate scores — one for each section. IRCC uses these individual scores (not an average) to assess your French proficiency. Different immigration programs have different minimum requirements per section.

nclc.title: NCLC/CLB Equivalence
nclc.desc: NCLC (Niveaux de compétence linguistique canadiens) is the French equivalent of CLB (Canadian Language Benchmarks). NCLC 7 equals CLB 7, which is the most commonly required level for Express Entry programs. For listening and reading, NCLC 7 corresponds to a score of approximately 458-502 out of 699. For speaking and writing, you need to be assessed at NCLC 7 or higher by the examiners.

immigration.title: Why TCF Canada Matters for Immigration
immigration.p1: French language proficiency is a significant advantage in the Canadian immigration process. Under the Express Entry Comprehensive Ranking System (CRS), strong French scores can earn you substantial additional points — up to 50 bonus points for CLB 7+ in French as a second official language, plus base language points.
immigration.p2: For the Programme régulier des travailleurs qualifiés (PRTQ) in Quebec, the TCF Canada is one of the accepted tests for demonstrating French proficiency. Many Provincial Nominee Programs (PNPs) also accept TCF Canada results. Achieving CLB 7+ in all four skills is the key benchmark that most applicants target.

clb7.heading: How to Reach CLB 7+ on TCF Canada
clb7.intro: CLB 7 (NCLC 7) is the most important benchmark for Canadian immigration. Reaching this level across all four skills can significantly boost your CRS score and open doors to permanent residence. This guide breaks down exactly what you need and how to get there.

whatIsClb7.title: What Does CLB 7 Mean?
whatIsClb7.p1: CLB 7 (Canadian Language Benchmark 7) represents an intermediate-to-advanced level of language proficiency. In the context of TCF Canada, it corresponds to NCLC 7. For the listening and reading sections, this typically requires a score of around 458-502 out of 699, which translates to roughly 78% accuracy across all difficulty levels.
whatIsClb7.p2: CLB 7+ in all four skills is the threshold used by IRCC for maximum Comprehensive Ranking System (CRS) points under Express Entry. Even if you are a primarily English-speaking applicant, demonstrating CLB 7+ in French as a second official language can earn you up to 50 additional CRS points — often the difference between receiving an Invitation to Apply (ITA) and waiting another round.

targetScores.title: Target Scores Per Section
targetScores.intro: Here are the approximate scores you need to reach NCLC 7 in each section:
targetScores.co: Listening (CO): 458-502 out of 699 — You need to correctly answer roughly 30 out of 39 questions, including most B1-B2 level questions.
targetScores.ce: Reading (CE): 458-502 out of 699 — Similar to listening, aim for about 30 correct answers with solid performance on mid-level texts.
targetScores.eo: Speaking (EO): NCLC 7 — Examiners assess fluency, vocabulary range, grammatical accuracy, pronunciation, and task completion across all 3 tasks.
targetScores.ee: Writing (EE): NCLC 7 — Your writing must demonstrate clear organization, varied vocabulary, generally accurate grammar, and appropriate register for each task type.

listening.title: Listening Strategies
listening.desc: Build a daily listening habit with authentic French audio: podcasts (RFI Journal en français facile, France Info), news broadcasts, and TCF practice recordings. Focus on understanding the main idea and key details on first listen, since the exam plays each recording only once. Use HiTCF's sentence-by-sentence replay feature to analyze passages you found difficult. Train your ear to distinguish French phonemes, liaisons, and enchaînements. Practice under timed conditions regularly to build stamina.

reading.title: Reading Strategies
reading.desc: Read French daily — newspapers (Le Monde, Le Devoir), magazines, and online articles covering various topics. For the exam, learn to identify key information quickly: scan for topic sentences, numbers, proper nouns, and logical connectors. Practice time management by allocating roughly 1.5 minutes per question. For higher-level texts (B2-C1), focus on understanding implicit meanings, author's tone, and argumentative structures. Build vocabulary systematically using flashcards and spaced repetition.

speaking.title: Speaking Strategies
speaking.desc: Practice speaking French every day, even if just 10 minutes of self-talk. For Task 1 (interview), prepare answers about your background, work, studies, and daily life. For Task 2 (role play), practice common scenarios: making complaints, negotiating, requesting information. For Task 3 (opinion), develop a framework: state your position, give 2-3 arguments with examples, acknowledge counterarguments, and conclude. Use HiTCF's AI conversation feature to simulate exam-like interactions and get instant feedback on pronunciation and fluency.

writing.title: Writing Strategies
writing.desc: Write something in French every day — even short journal entries count. For Task 1, master email conventions (formal vs. informal register, salutations, closing formulas). For Task 2, practice structuring articles with clear introductions, developed paragraphs, and conclusions. For Task 3, learn the comparative essay format: present each viewpoint fairly before giving your own opinion. Focus on grammatical accuracy (verb conjugations, agreements, prepositions) and vary your vocabulary. Always proofread your work — allocate 5 minutes at the end for corrections.

tcfListening.heading: TCF Canada Listening (CO) — Complete Practice Guide
tcfListening.intro: The Listening Comprehension section (Compréhension orale, or CO) is often considered the most challenging part of TCF Canada. Audio is played only once, and questions span from basic A1 dialogues to complex C2 academic discussions. This guide covers everything you need to know to maximize your score.

format.title: Exam Format
format.p1: The TCF Canada listening section consists of 39 multiple-choice questions with 4 answer options each (A, B, C, D). You have approximately 35 minutes to complete the section. Audio recordings are played only once — you cannot replay them during the actual exam.
format.p2: Questions are presented in order of increasing difficulty. The first questions test basic comprehension of simple exchanges, while the final questions require understanding nuanced arguments in academic or professional contexts. Each question is worth a different number of points based on its difficulty level, with harder questions worth significantly more.

a1a2.title: Level 1: A1-A2 (Questions 1-10)
a1a2.desc: Short everyday dialogues and announcements. You will hear simple conversations about daily topics — greetings, directions, shopping, appointments. The speech is slow and clearly articulated. Focus on identifying basic information: who, what, where, when. These questions are worth 3-9 points each and are the easiest to score on.

a2b1.title: Level 2: A2-B1 (Questions 11-19)
a2b1.desc: Longer dialogues and radio segments. Topics include travel arrangements, workplace conversations, phone calls, and public announcements. Speech is at a natural pace with some colloquial expressions. You need to understand the main message and specific details. These questions are worth 15 points each.

b1b2.title: Level 3: B1-B2 (Questions 20-30)
b1b2.desc: Extended conversations, interviews, and news reports. Topics become more abstract — social issues, cultural discussions, professional debates. Speakers may express opinions, make comparisons, and use figurative language. You need to identify the speaker's attitude, implicit meaning, and logical relationships. Worth 21 points each.

b2c1.title: Level 4: B2-C2 (Questions 31-39)
b2c1.desc: Complex academic lectures, expert panel discussions, and specialized reports. Topics include science, politics, philosophy, and economics. Speech is fast with sophisticated vocabulary and complex sentence structures. You need to follow extended arguments, understand subtle distinctions, and identify the overall thesis. Worth 27-33 points each — these questions can make or break your score.

tips.title: Listening Tips & Strategies
tips.tip1: Read the question and all 4 options before the audio plays. This gives you a preview of what to listen for and helps you focus your attention during playback.
tips.tip2: Don't panic if you miss something. Focus on the overall meaning rather than individual words. Many correct answers can be inferred from context even if you missed specific phrases.
tips.tip3: Pay attention to tone, emphasis, and intonation. Speakers often stress the most important information, and their tone can reveal attitudes that the question may ask about.
tips.tip4: Beware of distractors — options that use words you heard in the audio but in a different context or meaning. The correct answer often paraphrases the audio rather than repeating exact words.
tips.tip5: Practice with real French media daily. Listen to RFI, France 24, Radio-Canada, or French podcasts to build your ear for natural speech patterns, accents, and speed.

practice.title: How to Practice Effectively
practice.desc: On HiTCF, use Practice Mode to replay audio sentence by sentence — this builds deep comprehension. Once comfortable, switch to Exam Mode where audio plays once, just like the real test. Track your accuracy by level in the Dashboard to identify which difficulty bands need more work. Use the Level Practice feature to target specific levels (e.g., only B1-B2 questions) and the wrong-answer notebook to revisit your mistakes.

cta.desc: HiTCF has 42 complete listening test sets with over 1,600 questions, full audio, and sentence-level timestamps for detailed replay. Start for free and track your progress.

tcfReading.heading: TCF Canada Reading (CE) — Complete Practice Guide
tcfReading.intro: The Reading Comprehension section (Compréhension écrite, or CE) tests your ability to understand written French across a wide range of text types and difficulty levels. With 39 questions and 60 minutes on the clock, time management and strategic reading are essential skills.

format.title: Exam Format
format.p1: The TCF Canada reading section consists of 39 multiple-choice questions with 4 answer options each. You have approximately 60 minutes to complete all questions. Unlike listening, you can go back and change your answers, so use your time wisely.
format.p2: Questions are arranged in order of increasing difficulty, from simple everyday texts to complex academic and literary passages. The scoring system gives more weight to harder questions, so maintaining accuracy on mid-level questions while attempting the advanced ones is the optimal strategy.

a1a2.title: Level 1: A1-A2 (Questions 1-10)
a1a2.desc: Short, practical texts: signs, labels, simple ads, brief messages, and menus. Questions ask you to identify basic information such as prices, dates, locations, and simple instructions. The vocabulary is everyday and concrete. These are quick wins — aim for 100% accuracy here.

a2b1.title: Level 2: A2-B1 (Questions 11-19)
a2b1.desc: Medium-length texts: personal emails, blog posts, travel guides, product descriptions, and simple news articles. You need to understand the main idea, locate specific information, and follow a basic narrative or argument. Some vocabulary may be unfamiliar, but context clues are usually available.

b1b2.title: Level 3: B1-B2 (Questions 20-30)
b1b2.desc: Longer, more complex texts: newspaper editorials, magazine features, formal correspondence, and informational brochures. Questions test your ability to understand implicit meaning, identify the author's purpose and tone, and follow logical arguments. Vocabulary is more abstract and sentences are longer with subordinate clauses.

b2c1.title: Level 4: B2-C2 (Questions 31-39)
b2c1.desc: Sophisticated texts: academic papers, literary excerpts, legal documents, philosophical essays, and scientific reports. You need to analyze complex arguments, understand nuanced vocabulary, identify rhetorical devices, and synthesize information across paragraphs. These texts often contain specialized terminology and dense sentence structures.

tips.title: Reading Tips & Strategies
tips.tip1: Read the question first, then the text. This focused reading approach saves time and helps you locate relevant information quickly instead of reading the entire passage in detail.
tips.tip2: For longer texts, skim first to get the overall structure and main idea, then read more carefully around the sections relevant to the questions. Pay attention to paragraph beginnings and topic sentences.
tips.tip3: Watch out for negation and qualifiers. Words like ne...pas, ne...jamais, seulement, rarement, and à peine can completely change the meaning of a sentence and are commonly used in distractors.
tips.tip4: Build your vocabulary systematically. Learn word families (e.g., emploi, employeur, employé, employer) and common prefixes/suffixes (re-, dé-, -ment, -tion) to help you deduce meanings of unfamiliar words.
tips.tip5: Time management is crucial. Spend about 1 minute on A1-A2 questions, 1.5 minutes on B1 questions, and up to 2 minutes on B2-C2 questions. If stuck, mark the question and move on.

practice.title: How to Practice Effectively
practice.desc: On HiTCF, start with Practice Mode to read at your own pace and review explanations for each question. Progress to Exam Mode with the 60-minute timer to simulate real test conditions. Use the Dashboard to track your accuracy by difficulty level and the Level Practice feature to focus specifically on the levels where you need improvement. Review your mistakes in the wrong-answer notebook and look for patterns in the types of questions you get wrong.

tcfSpeaking.heading: TCF Canada Speaking (EO) — Complete Practice Guide
tcfSpeaking.intro: The Speaking section (Expression orale, or EO) is a face-to-face interview lasting approximately 12 minutes, recorded and evaluated by certified examiners. It tests your ability to communicate in French across three progressively challenging tasks. Many candidates find this the most stressful section, but structured preparation can make a huge difference.

format.title: Exam Structure: 3 Tasks
format.p1: The speaking exam consists of three tasks of increasing difficulty. You interact with an examiner who guides the conversation. The entire session is audio-recorded for evaluation by multiple raters.

task1.title: Task 1: Guided Interview (2 minutes)
task1.desc: The examiner asks you questions about yourself — your background, daily life, interests, work, and studies. This is the most predictable section and targets A1-B1 level. You should answer in complete sentences, provide details and examples, and show you can sustain a basic conversation. Prepare answers for common topics: family, hometown, hobbies, job/studies, daily routine, and future plans.

task2.title: Task 2: Role Play (3.5 minutes)
task2.desc: You receive a scenario card describing a situation where you must interact with the examiner (who plays a specific role). Common scenarios include: making a complaint at a store, negotiating with a landlord, resolving a workplace conflict, or organizing an event. This targets B1-B2 level. You need to be persuasive, handle unexpected responses, and adapt your language to the situation.

task3.title: Task 3: Expressing a Point of View (4.5 minutes)
task3.desc: You are given a topic or document and must express and defend your opinion. The examiner will challenge your views with counter-arguments. This targets B2-C2 level. You need to structure your argument logically, provide concrete examples, acknowledge opposing viewpoints, and use a wide range of vocabulary and grammatical structures. Topics typically involve social, cultural, or environmental issues.

scoring.title: How Speaking Is Scored
scoring.p1: Examiners evaluate your performance on a grid covering: lexical range and accuracy, grammatical range and accuracy, fluency and coherence, pronunciation and intonation, and sociolinguistic appropriateness. Each criterion contributes to your overall NCLC level.
scoring.p2: To reach NCLC 7 in speaking, you need to demonstrate: ability to discuss abstract topics with reasonable fluency, use a range of vocabulary accurately, produce complex sentences with generally correct grammar, have clear pronunciation with appropriate intonation, and adapt your register to different situations.

tips.title: Speaking Tips & Strategies
tips.tip1: Speak clearly and at a natural pace. It is better to speak slowly and correctly than quickly with many errors. Examiners are trained to evaluate quality, not speed.
tips.tip2: Use discourse markers and connectors to structure your speech: d'abord, ensuite, en revanche, par conséquent, cependant. This makes your speech sound more organized and raises your coherence score.
tips.tip3: Don't be afraid to self-correct. If you realize you made a grammatical mistake, briefly correct yourself and move on. This shows awareness of accuracy and is viewed positively by examiners.
tips.tip4: For Task 3, always structure your response: introduce your position, give 2-3 arguments with examples, address the counterargument, and conclude. Even if your French isn't perfect, a well-structured response scores higher.
tips.tip5: Practice speaking aloud daily. Record yourself, listen back, and identify areas for improvement. Use HiTCF's AI conversation feature to simulate the examiner interaction and get instant feedback on pronunciation and fluency.

preparation.title: Effective Preparation Methods
preparation.p1: Use HiTCF's pronunciation practice to record yourself reading French texts and get real-time AI assessment of your accuracy and fluency at the phoneme level. This builds the muscle memory and confidence you need for the actual exam.
preparation.p2: For conversational practice, HiTCF's AI Conversation feature simulates Tâche 2 and Tâche 3 with an AI examiner. You practice speaking on real exam topics, receive 6-dimension scoring, and can repeat scenarios to improve. This is the closest experience to the real exam you can get from home.

tcfWriting.heading: TCF Canada Writing (EE) — Complete Practice Guide
tcfWriting.intro: The Writing section (Expression écrite, or EE) requires you to produce three written texts of increasing complexity in 60 minutes. Unlike the multiple-choice sections, writing is evaluated holistically by trained examiners, so understanding the scoring criteria and mastering the right methodology is key to a high score.

format.title: Exam Structure: 3 Tasks
format.p1: The writing exam consists of three tasks that test progressively more advanced writing skills. You write on paper (or on a computer at some test centers), and your responses are evaluated by multiple certified examiners.

task1.title: Task 1: Short Message (60-120 words, ~15 minutes)
task1.desc: Write a message, email, or short letter in response to a given situation. Common scenarios include: responding to an invitation, writing to a landlord about a maintenance issue, contacting a colleague about a schedule change, or replying to a job listing. This targets A1-B1 level. Focus on: using the appropriate register (tu/vous), including all required information, and following standard French letter/email conventions.

task2.title: Task 2: Article or Formal Letter (120-150 words, ~20 minutes)
task2.desc: Write an article, blog post, or formal letter on a given topic. You may be asked to describe an experience, explain a phenomenon, or argue for a position. This targets B1-B2 level. You need a clear introduction, well-developed body paragraphs, and a conclusion. Demonstrate varied vocabulary, accurate grammar (past tenses, subjunctive, conditional), and logical organization with appropriate connectors.

task3.title: Task 3: Comparative Essay (120-180 words, ~25 minutes)
task3.desc: Analyze and compare two perspectives on a given topic, then express your own opinion. You typically receive two short texts presenting opposing viewpoints (e.g., remote work vs. office work, technology in education). This targets B2-C2 level. You must: summarize each viewpoint fairly, identify key similarities and differences, present your own reasoned opinion with evidence, and write a balanced conclusion. This is where strong argumentation and sophisticated language use matter most.

scoring.title: How Writing Is Scored
scoring.p1: Examiners evaluate each task on criteria including: sociolinguistic competence (appropriate register, conventions), lexical competence (vocabulary range and precision), grammatical competence (accuracy and complexity of structures), and pragmatic/discourse competence (organization, coherence, argumentation).
scoring.p2: To reach NCLC 7 in writing, you need to demonstrate: clear text organization with logical paragraph structure, varied vocabulary used accurately, generally correct grammar with complex structures (subjunctive, conditional, relative clauses), appropriate register for each task type, and the ability to develop and support an argument.

tips.title: Writing Tips & Strategies
tips.tip1: Plan before you write. Spend 2-3 minutes outlining your main points for each task. A well-organized response with simple French scores higher than a disorganized response with complex French.
tips.tip2: Respect the word count. Writing significantly under or over the limit can affect your score. For Task 1, aim for 80-100 words; for Task 2, 130-140 words; for Task 3, 150-170 words.
tips.tip3: Use varied connectors: cependant, néanmoins, en outre, par ailleurs, en effet, de surcroît, toutefois. This shows lexical range and improves the coherence of your text.
tips.tip4: Show off your grammar range deliberately. Use at least one conditional (Si j'avais...), one subjunctive (Il est important que...), and relative clauses (ce qui/ce que) in your longer tasks. Examiners look for evidence of complex structures.
tips.tip5: Always proofread. Reserve 5 minutes at the end to check for: gender/number agreements, verb conjugation errors, missing accents, and unclear sentences. Catching 3-4 errors in proofreading can raise your score by a full NCLC level.

methodology.title: Methodology for Each Task Type
methodology.p1: For Task 1 (message), use a clear template: greeting appropriate to the context, state the purpose of your message, provide necessary details or requests, and close with an appropriate formula (Cordialement, Bien à vous, Amicalement). For Task 2 (article/letter), follow the introduction-body-conclusion structure: hook the reader, develop 2-3 points with examples, and wrap up with a memorable closing statement.
methodology.p2: For Task 3 (comparative essay), use this proven structure: Introduction (present the topic and announce your plan) -> First viewpoint (summarize and comment) -> Second viewpoint (summarize and comment) -> Your opinion (with reasons and examples) -> Conclusion (synthesize and open). Master this framework and you can apply it to any topic.
